XSS Attacks: The Frontend Security Bug You Really Should Know About
Understanding XSS attacks is one of those things every frontend developer really should know about. So I figured I'd take some notes on what XSS actually is.
XSS stands for Cross-Site Scripting. The problem almost always comes down to JavaScript, and a couple of the more common tricks look like this:
Redirecting you to someone else's site
Grabbing the username and password from a login page and sending them off to someone else's server
XSS roughly breaks down into three types.
The easy way to picture persistent XSS is someone slipping a snippet like
while(i < 10){ alert('Haha, you just got XSS-attacked!'); i++ }

onto your homepage, so it keeps bugging you over and over. The thing to watch here is that this kind of XSS gets stored in your database. So when you're inserting data, you really need to be careful about whatever the user typed in — otherwise this XSS attack just keeps firing forever.

Reflected XSS usually happens when the server sends data back to the frontend. The classic case is a GET request: the frontend asks the backend for data, the backend doesn't bother checking whether that data is safe, and just spits it straight back. That's when XSS shows up — because the data coming back might look like this:

(My company actually got hit by a DOM XSS attack recently.)

Understanding XSS attacks is one of those things every frontend developer really should know. So here are my notes on what XSS actually is — the three main types (stored, reflected, and DOM-based), and a couple of simple ways to defend against it on the frontend, including why `createElement` can be safer than `innerHTML`.
